preFix.title: Dyeing with heather
preFix.subtitle: Using Calluna vulgaris flowering tips with alum mordant to dye wool a soft yellow-green, or a golden-brown without mordant
preFix.excerpt: Common heather (Calluna vulgaris) gives alum-mordanted wool a soft yellow-green in late summer when the flowering tips are at their most dye-rich. Unmordanted wool from the same bath takes a golden-brown. Heather grows abundantly on British upland moorland and requires no specialist sourcing. The dye bath needs no long extraction and is suitable as an early natural-dyeing project.
preFix.sourceNotes: Pellew, Charles Livingston. Dyes and Dyeing. New York, 1913. Public domain. Internet Archive. Hummel, J. J. The Dyeing of Textile Fabrics. London, 1885. Public domain. Internet Archive. Both sources document Calluna vulgaris colour range on alum-mordanted and unmordanted wool.

attrs.body: Dye-pots are dedicated and never return to food use. Use stainless steel or enamelled pots. Wear gloves and apron. Work with extractor running or outdoors.
attrs.title: Mordant safety

content.text: wool a soft yellow-green. The same

on unmordanted wool gives a golden-brown, as the dye attaches by surface affinity rather than mordant bond and shifts toward the warmer end of its range. The dye comes from the flowering tips, which are at their most concentrated from late July through September in Britain, when the moorland turns purple-pink.

Heather is one of the most accessible British dye plants: it grows widely on upland moorland, heathland, and acid grassland across Scotland, northern England, Wales, and parts of Ireland. Collect flowering tips with scissors, taking no more than a small fraction of any stand. Fresh tips give slightly more dye than dried, but dried heather from late summer foraging works well through winter.

items.name: Wool yarn or fleece, dry weight
Heather flowering tips (200% weight of fibre)
Stainless steel dye-pot, dedicated to dyeing
Fine-mesh sieve or muslin for straining

content.text: Submerge the wool in cool water and leave for at least 30 minutes. Pre-wetting allows even mordant uptake throughout the skein. Squeeze gently without wringing before moving the wool to the mordant bath.

Dissolve 25 g of alum potash in 200 ml of hot water, then stir into a pot of warm water large enough for the wool to move freely. Lower the pre-wetted wool in. Bring to 80°C over 30 minutes and hold at 80°C for 45 minutes, turning the wool every 10 minutes. Remove and set aside without rinsing. The mordant bath can be cooled and kept for one further use.

Pack the heather tips loosely into the dye-pot and cover with water. Bring to 80°C and hold for 45 minutes, pressing the tips occasionally with a stirring spoon. The bath will turn a golden-yellow to greenish-yellow depending on the heather source. Strain through a fine-mesh sieve, pressing the plant material firmly. Discard the spent tips. Return the liquid to the pot.

Lower the mordanted wool into the strained dye bath. Bring to 80°C over 20 minutes and hold for 60 minutes, turning the wool every 15 minutes. Lift a small section at 45 minutes and rinse quickly under cool water to check the colour: a soft yellow-green on alum-mordanted wool. The wet colour appears darker than the dried result. Remove the wool once the colour looks right.

Lift the wool from the dye bath and cool slightly. Rinse in water at a similar temperature until the rinse water runs clear. Squeeze gently without wringing. Dry in shade. The yellow-green on alum-mordanted wool softens gradually over time but is reasonably stable for a plant dye.

Alum mordant (25% weight of fibre) gives the characteristic soft yellow-green on wool. Using copper sulphate mordant (2% weight of fibre) in place of alum gives a deeper, greener result with more yellow removed from it, moving toward a moss tone. Iron mordant (2% weight of fibre) shifts the result toward a grey-olive or khaki. Unmordanted wool in the same bath takes a golden-brown by surface affinity: warm but less clear than the mordanted result.

items.fix: Use heather at 200% weight of fibre minimum. Collect in August to September at peak bloom. Post-bloom dried tips give significantly less colour.
items.cause: Heather was harvested too late in the season after blooming, or insufficient plant material was used
items.problem: Colour is very pale on the mordanted skein

items.fix: Maintain the mordant bath at 80°C for the full 45 minutes. Hold the dye bath at 80°C for the complete 60 minutes. Below 75°C the green component in the colour does not fully develop.
items.cause: Mordant bath temperature was too low, or the dye bath was not held at 80°C long enough
items.problem: Yellow-green came out more yellow than green

items.fix: Use a pot large enough for the fibre to move without bunching. Turn the wool every 15 minutes throughout the dye bath. Crowded dyeing gives consistently uneven results.
items.cause: Wool was crowded in the pot and could not move freely

content.text: Heather with alum mordant is low-hazard at home quantities. The spent dye bath and mordant bath can both go down a foul drain once cooled. The spent plant material is organic and can go in a garden compost heap or food-waste bin. If an iron or copper modifier was used, that bath must go down a foul drain only: never into a storm drain, watercourse, or soakaway.
